End-to-end encryption: Ensuring data is encrypted at the source and remains encrypted throughout its lifecycle.
Differential Privacy: Adding statistical noise to the data to protect individual identities while still allowing for useful analysis.
Federated Learning: Training the AI model on decentralized data sources without actually transferring the data itself.
Independent Data Audits: Regular, unannounced audits by a third-party privacy organization.
Data Minimization: Collecting only the data absolutely necessary for therapeutic purposes.

These safeguards are unlikely to be fully implemented – or rigorously enforced – in practice for several reasons: * Cost: Implementing these measures is expensive, and large healthcare providers will prioritize cost-cutting. * Complexity: They require specialized expertise and ongoing maintenance. * Usability: Some safeguards, like differential privacy, can reduce the accuracy of the AI model, making it less effective. * Competitive Advantage: Providers may see unencrypted, readily-analyzable data as a competitive advantage. * Lack of Enforcement: Regulatory oversight in this area is still nascent and often lacks teeth.
Prioritizing scale over privacy sends a dangerous message that undermines the very foundation of trust required for long-term sustainability.
A smaller, more controlled release allows us to demonstrate that privacy and innovation can coexist.
